title: Discipleship 12: The Role of Parents in Discipleship — Worship (cont'd)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 12 - The Role of Parents in Discipleship - Worship (cont’d) - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction and Review
subpoints: Discipleship is the mission of the church; raising children in the faith is a major part of that
God has made promises to us as believers and to our children — this motivates careful instruction
Review: children must be in the worship of God; the Bible consistently commands and models this from the Abrahamic covenant through the New Testament
One sad biblical example of what happens when a generation fails to train the next: Judges 2:10

outline.title: Children Are Holy — Members of the Body of Christ
subpoints: 1 Corinthians 1:2-3: Paul writes 'to the church of God... to those who are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints'
1 Corinthians 7:14: 'otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy' — the word 'holy' is the same Greek word as 'saints' in chapter 1
Therefore the children of believers are saints — members of the church Paul is writing to
This does not guarantee heaven, but it means they are in a special covenantal relationship with God
They are part of the body of Christ — this is the ground for including them in the corporate worship of the body

outline.title: Why We Don't Include Children: Three Failures of Faith (Continued)
subpoints: We don't believe what the Bible says about the importance of corporate worship — fruit reveals this (attendance patterns)
We don't believe what the Bible says about preaching and regeneration: the argument 'they can't understand' misunderstands the new birth
Regeneration precedes faith and understanding; the preached Word is the instrument God uses to give life to the spiritually dead
Ezekiel 37 reviewed at length: God commands preaching to dry bones — through the preached Word, the Spirit gives life
The church must never remove children from the very means God has appointed for their spiritual life
A third reason: we do not take the time to train ourselves and our children for worship — it is hard work that must begin at home, not on Sunday

outline.title: Getting Better: Right Belief, Right Love, Right Focus
subpoints: Right belief: whatever God says, we believe; the preached Word is powerful regardless of cognitive ability; the corporate worship of God is the most glorious thing we can do in this life
Right love — it's not all about us: people without young children must be patient; people with young children must do the work of training and minimizing distraction for those around them
Right focus: we are the body of Christ; children are members of the body; we worship as a whole, excluding no age group
Practical for parents with young children: train at home all week; talk through worship before it happens; discipline when needed, then bring children back
Practical for those without young children: pray for the children specifically; sit near families to help; do not distract children with faces or waves

outline.title: Practical Tips for Worship Preparation and Participation
subpoints: Pray before worship: for the service, for the sermon, for the children, for growth
Arrive early; find a good seat; silence your phone
Avoid unnecessary movement and conversation during the service
Use the offering time as a time of personal prayer and dedication
Engage actively in every element of worship — singing, prayer, reading, sacraments, preaching
Explain each element to your children during the service in real time
God blesses faithfulness — always, even when we cannot always see the blessing

summary:
This session is a direct continuation of the previous lesson on children in corporate worship, completing the case and adding practical guidance. Pastor Lyro begins with a review of why children should be present in worship — from the Abrahamic covenant through the New Testament — and adds a brief but weighty warning from Judges 2:10, where the generation that saw God's mighty acts failed to pass them on and the next generation served other gods.

A fresh exegetical argument is introduced: in 1 Corinthians 1:2-3, Paul writes to 'the saints' at Corinth. By the time he reaches chapter 7:14, he says the children of at least one believing parent are 'holy' — and the word is the same Greek word used for 'saints' in chapter 1. Therefore, the children of believers are saints in the New Testament sense — members of the church Paul is addressing. This is the theological ground for their presence in the gathered body: they are not observers but participants.

The three failures of faith addressed in the previous lesson (disbelief about the importance of worship; disbelief about preaching and regeneration; failure to do the work of training) are continued and deepened here. The Ezekiel 37 passage receives extended exposition: God commanded Ezekiel to preach to dry, dead bones — and through the preached Word, God's Spirit gave life. The conclusion: if God can give life to dead bones through preaching, he can give life to a child who does not yet understand. To remove children from the preaching of the Word is to remove them from the very means God has appointed for their regeneration.

The session concludes with specific practical guidance for the whole congregation. For parents: training begins at home; explain each element of worship to children in real time; discipline and return rather than removing for the whole service. For those without young children: pray specifically for the covenant children; help families; resist the urge to distract children. The final word is an exhortation to active, expectant engagement in every element of worship, trusting that God blesses faithfulness.
transcript:
## Review: Why Children Belong in Corporate Worship

We are continuing the section of our discipleship series on children in public corporate worship. Last week we established from Scripture that children have always been part of God's assembled people. We saw this in:

- The Abrahamic covenant, which included children and gave them the sign of circumcision
- The Old Testament worship assemblies, where men, women, little ones, and even infants were called to be present (Deuteronomy 29, 31; Joshua 8; Joel 2)
- Paul's letters to the Ephesians and Colossians, which directly address children as members of the congregation — meaning Paul expected them to be present when these letters were read aloud in the service
- Jesus himself, who was indignant when his disciples tried to keep children from him: *"Let the little children come to Me, and do not forbid them."* (Mark 10:14)

We also addressed the theological argument against children in worship — "they can't understand" — and showed that regeneration has nothing to do with cognitive ability. God gives new hearts through the preaching of the word, before understanding, not because of it. Ezekiel 37 makes this vivid: through the prophet's preaching, dead dry bones came to life. Can God not do the same for a child through the preaching of his word?

## Why Are the Children Not Pagans?

This week, before we turn to practical matters, I want to address a question of status: are our covenant children little pagans until they profess faith, or are they something else?

Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 7:14:

> *"For the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified by the husband; otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy."*

The word "holy" here is the same word Paul uses in chapter 1 for the "saints" to whom he is writing. The children of believing parents are *holy* — set apart, covenantally sanctified — even before they have professed personal faith. They are in the church. They are not outside, waiting to be admitted.

This does not mean every covenant child will inevitably be saved. The New Testament is clear that there are those who are in the church but not of it — branches that are in the vine but bear no fruit and are cut off (John 15). But it means the children have a special relationship with God through the faith of their parents that children outside the covenant do not have. They are under the preaching, they observe the sacraments, they are discipled by the body of Christ, they are covered by the prayers of the assembly.

We raise them accordingly. We teach them to pray to God as their Father. We teach them to follow the commands of Christ. We do not treat them as if they have no relationship with God — and we do not hold back the gospel from them as if they have no need of it. We press the gospel upon them precisely *because* they belong to God.

## Right Belief, Right Love, Right Focus (Review)

Last week we said that getting better at having children in worship requires:

**Right belief** — trusting what God says about corporate worship, preaching, and regeneration.

**Right love** — thinking of others, not just ourselves.

**Right focus** — remembering that we are the whole body of Christ, and the children are members of that body.

## Practical Help for Families with Young Children

Training children for worship does not begin on Sunday — it begins on Monday. If we do not expect them to sit and listen at home during family devotions, we cannot expect it in the sanctuary. Obedience in worship flows from obedience at home.

**Before the service**: Walk your children through what will happen. Explain each element — the singing, the prayers, the reading, the sermon, the offering, the Lord's Supper. Remind them that we save our best for the house of God. Take care of all physical needs — water, a trip to the restroom, snacks — before worship begins. The service is typically an hour to an hour and ten minutes; no one's bladder will explode.

**During the service**: Quietly narrate what is happening for younger children. *"Now we are praying because we depend on God alone. Now we are singing praise to the One who saved us. Now we are hearing the word of God, because this is how God speaks to us."* When a child is chattering disobediently after you have asked them to be quiet, take them out, address their behavior, and bring them back. The sanctuary is not the place to discipline — take them out and return.

**Consistency matters**: Children who know exactly what is expected of them each week are far more likely to settle into worship than children who face shifting expectations. What you patiently and consistently require, they will eventually give you.

## Practical Help for Those Without Young Children

Your role matters too. Be patient with families who are in the hard years of training. Pray for the children of this church specifically, throughout the week, that they would come to know and love the Lord Jesus Christ. Consider sitting near a family with multiple young children and gently helping to guide the little ones when parents cannot reach them.

And please: do not distract the children by waving at them or making faces during the service. It sounds amusing, but it is genuinely counterproductive to what their parents are trying to do. Be part of the training, not an obstacle to it.

## We Are the Body of Christ

When we gather for worship, we gather as the whole body — from the oldest member to the youngest, even the nursing infants. That is the picture of heaven. That is the church that Christ loved and gave himself for. Let us worship him together, all of us, as one.

> *"We will not hide them from their children, telling to the generation to come the praises of the Lord, and His strength and His wonderful works that He has done."* (Psalm 78:4)

God blesses faithfulness. He will bring our children to himself as we faithfully and lovingly include them in the worship of the Lord.
